sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Neurogenic shock is a distributive shock resulting from acute spinal cord injury (SCI) or central nervous system (CNS) trauma, leading to loss of sympathetic tone, systemic vasodilation, and impaired haemodynamic stability. It is characterised by hypotension, bradycardia, and warm peripheries due to unopposed parasympathetic activity.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Hypotension: Systolic BP <90 mmHg or >30% below baseline
- Bradycardia: Heart rate <60 bpm (unlike hypovolemic shock, which typically causes tachycardia)
- Warm, dry and flushed skin due to peripheral vasodilation, especially below level of injury)
- Possible hypothermia due to vasodilation
- Respiratory distress (if injury involves cervical spine or brainstem)
- Altered mental status (confusion, lethargy)
- Priapism (in males) or loss of reflexes

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Consider and exclude other causes of shock, especially haemorrhage, before assuming neurogenic shock
- Be alert to respiratory compromise, especially with cervical cord injury
- Patients may appear peripherally warm but still be centrally underperfused
- Beware of fluid overload; hypotension in neurogenic shock is not usually corrected by repeated large fluid boluses
- Neurogenic shock must be differentiated from spinal shock because their management differs
- Spinal shock refers to neurological dysfunction below the injury level, whereas neurogenic shock refers to haemodynamic instability with hypotension and often bradycardia
- Maintain spinal motion restriction according to the trauma context
- Vagal stimuli such as suctioning or airway instrumentation may worsen bradycardia

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Consider ventilatory support if necessary
- Give oxygen if clinically indicated
- Maintain spinal motion restriction and careful handling
- Perform frequent vital signs
- Keep the patient warm because temperature regulation may be impaired
- Consider Secondary or CNS Survey
- Consider early consultation with RFDS or senior retrieval support

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Apply cardiac monitoring
- Establish vascular access
- Give IV fluid cautiously while reassessing for occult haemorrhage and signs of overload
- Avoid hypotension; where acute spinal cord injury is suspected, discuss haemodynamic targets early with the on-call doctor or RFDS
- Consider atropine in consultation with the on-call doctor for clinically significant bradycardia
- Consider analgesia and antiemetic therapy where appropriate
- Consider vasopressor support in persistent hypotension only in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Insert urinary catheter only if within scope, clinically needed, and logistically appropriate

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Ensure patient is correctly packed to be received by RFDS, i.e., Patient is immobilised within a vacuum mattress where available
